one_line_takeaway: CATMARK makes LLM watermarking work across mixed code/text outputs by clustering tokens on their logits and setting a per-cluster, quantile-based entropy threshold automatically, achieving top-tier detection (up to 100% AUROC/TPR on StackEval, 97.0% AUROC on HumanEval) without sacrificing pass@1.
key_finding: CATMARK simultaneously matches or beats baselines on both output quality and detection across all four cross-task benchmarks, breaking the quality-vs-detectability trade-off that traps static-threshold methods (SWEET-1.2 keeps quality but loses detection on code; SWEET-0.6 the reverse). Highlights: HumanEval pass@1 82.3% (tied best) with AUROC 97.0% and TPR 82.9% (both best); StackEval reaches 100% AUROC and 100% TPR while raising the judged average score to 2.72 (vs ~2.3 for baselines) and acceptance rate to 97.5%. It also degrades less under rewriting attacks than baselines and adds only modest overhead.
core_contribution: CATMARK (Context-Aware Threshold Watermarking), a decoding-time LLM watermarking framework that replaces a single static entropy threshold with per-context, dynamically computed thresholds so a watermark can be embedded robustly in mixed-modality outputs (e.g., low-entropy code interleaved with high-entropy natural-language comments) without degrading functional correctness. Its own novel pieces are: (1) online token categorization that clusters each step's logit vector to learned prototypes via negative KL divergence (new cluster spawned when max similarity d_k* < alpha; prototype updated by cumulative moving average); (2) an automatic per-cluster entropy threshold tau_k set to the quantile of that cluster's historical entropy at cumulative probability q = f(mean_entropy) with f(x)=e^{-x}, so no manual/task-specific threshold tuning is needed; (3) an EWD-style entropy-weighted detector (weight w(H)=H over tokens with H>tau) plus a theoretical result (Theorem 1) proving CATMARK's z-score lower bound exceeds EWD's by excluding low-entropy tokens that satisfy S_j < gamma + (1-gamma)e^{-delta} and thus contribute negatively to the detection signal. The red/green-list bias mechanism itself is inherited from KGW (Kirchenbauer et al.), not claimed as new.
threat_model: Provenance/attribution setting: a model provider embeds an imperceptible statistical watermark (green-list logit bias delta with secret key) into LLM output so machine-generated text can later be detected, supporting authenticity verification and misuse prevention. The detector is the defender; the adversary is a text rewriter who tries to strip the watermark before detection. The paper evaluates two concrete removal attacks: back-translation (English->French->English) and paraphrasing with a smaller Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct model. The authors also flag (but do not defend against) a future threat: redundancy-injection attacks that artificially inflate entropy to fool the adaptive threshold.
limitations: Only two Qwen 14B models are tested, so cross-model/architecture generalization is unverified despite generalization being a stated motivation. Robustness is not absolute: under back-translation CATMARK's AUROC falls from 0.998 to 0.916 and under paraphrasing to 0.934 (still best among methods, but a real drop). The authors explicitly concede vulnerability to redundancy-injection attacks that inflate entropy, which is not mitigated. Detection assumes white-box access to model logits to recompute entropy, and the whole approach inherits KGW's green/red-list paradigm (distortionary bias) rather than being distortion-free. The threshold mapping f(x)=e^{-x} is chosen empirically; the reciprocal variant collapses to 0% TPR, indicating sensitivity to this design choice. Generation is ~5.4% slower than baselines.

datasets_used: HumanEval (Python code generation + line-by-line comment generation)
StackEval (first 500 Intermediate-level Stack Overflow questions, LLM-as-a-Judge)
quantitative_results: HumanEval pass@1: CATMARK 82.3 (tied best with SWEET-1.2) vs KGW 74.4, EWD 74.6
HumanEval AUROC: CATMARK 97.0 (best) vs EWD 96.4, SWEET-0.6 94.5, KGW 73.4
HumanEval TPR (FPR<5%): CATMARK 82.9 (best) vs EWD 81.7, SWEET-0.6 67.7
MBPP: CATMARK pass@1 51.6, AUROC 93.4, TPR 67.2 (all best among methods)
MATH-500: CATMARK pass@1 71.6 (best), AUROC 99.8 (tied EWD), TPR 99.0 (tied EWD)
StackEval: CATMARK judged avg score 2.72 vs SWEET 2.32/KGW 2.28; acceptance rate 97.5 vs ~92; AUROC 100.0 and TPR 100.0 (both best)
Threshold-function ablation (HumanEval): e^{-x} best (AUROC 97.0, pass@1 82.9); reciprocal x^{-1} fails at 0.0% TPR / 50.0 AUROC
Robustness: CATMARK (aetw) AUROC 0.998 -> 0.916 under back-translation, 0.998 -> 0.934 under paraphrasing (highest post-attack among all methods)
Compute: 33.1 tokens/s generation (5.4% slower than baselines; SWEET 35.1, EWD 36.3, KGW/WLLM 36.8); detection 1.017 s/sample, faster than EWD's 1.031 s
Hyperparameter stability: coefficient of variation Cv < 1% across tested alpha/rho grid (max 0.96% on MBPP AUROC)
